title: CAR-T-cell therapy might prevent bone-marrow cancer
html_body:
What matters in cancer research |
View this email in your browser Thursday 30 April 2026

Hello Nature readers,
This week, we hear that CAR-T-cell therapy might prevent bone-marrow cancer, learn how a beating heart stops cancer from growing inside it and discover that there are more microplastics in brains with tumours.
The antibody therapy Darzalex is the only approved treatment for high-risk smoldering myeloma, a precancerous blood condition. Even with this treatment, many people with this condition develop bone-marrow cancer within five years, so there is a need for better therapies. (Medicimage/Science Photo Library)

CAR T might prevent bone-marrow cancer

Twenty people with smoldering multiple myeloma who were at high risk of developing bone-marrow cancer had no detectable abnormal white blood cells after treatment with CAR-T-cell therapy. The potential side effects from CAR T, such as neurotoxicity, need to be weighed against the benefits, because people with smoldering multiple myeloma don’t typically have symptoms. Alison Cameron, an anesthesiologist who participated in the trial and experienced serious immune-system side effects, says potentially never needing treatment again is “well worth everything I went through”.

STAT | 7 min read
 Reference: Nature Medicine paper (20 April)

The pump of the heart ‘beats’ cancer

In mice, the mechanical action of the heart beating stops tumours from growing — which could explain why heart cancers are so rare in all mammals. Researchers compared a non-beating heart that had been transplanted onto the necks of mice with the ‘native’ hearts in the animals. After being injected with cancer cells, the external hearts were swiftly taken over by the disease, while the beating hearts stayed much closer to cancer-free. But there is a downside: pumping also seems to stop the heart from regenerating like other tissue can.

Nature | 4 min read
 Reference: Science paper (23 April)

In the news

  • Breast cancer is less likely to spread to the lungs of mice that are infected with respiratory syncytial virus (RSV), a virus that causes cold-like symptoms. Researchers found that the lungs produced proteins called type I interferons to prevent the replication of RSV and that these proteins stopped metastatic breast cancer cells from taking hold in the lining of the lungs. (New Scientist | 5 min read) Reference: PNAS paper (17 April)
  • Pharmaceutical company Eli Lilly will acquire the biotechnology company Kelonia Therapeutics for at least US$3.25 billion, along with its next-generation treatment for the blood cancer multiple myeloma. (The Wall Street Journal | 1 min read - paywall)

ADVERTISEMENT FEATURE
Paediatric cancers are not biologically the same as their adult counterparts — meaning they need their own research and treatments. (St. Jude Children’s Research Hospital)

A new playbook for childhood cancers

St. Jude researchers are calling attention to a simple but overlooked fact: children’s cancers aren’t just smaller versions of adult disease. Many paediatric tumours arise from disrupted developmental processes and carry far fewer mutations, meaning the timing and cell context play a critical role in disease onset. Those differences explain why treatments built for adult cancers often fall short in young patients. The team argues for models, data and therapies designed specifically for children — and shows that understanding developmental biology is key to creating more precise, effective care for them.

Reference: Nature Reviews Drug Discovery paper, Nature Communications paper & Cancer Cell paper

Must-read papers

Herbicide tied to colorectal cancer in youth

People under the age of 50 are more likely to develop colorectal cancer if they’ve been exposed to the herbicide picloram. Researchers examined patterns of chemical tags called methyl groups on DNA in colorectal tumour samples and found that under 50s with the disease were more likely to have epigenetic changes tied to picloram exposure than were older adults.

Reference: Nature Medicine paper (21 April)

Why one cancer type is so common in China

Around half of all cases of head and neck cancer globally occur in people living in southern China, and an interaction between a strain of the Epstein–Barr virus and a gene variant prevalent in that population could explain the anomaly. People with both risk factors make up around 21% of the population in southern China and account for 47% of the nasopharyngeal carcinoma cases. “Our results show that nasopharyngeal carcinoma risk is shaped not by host and viral variants independently, but by their synergistic interactions,” report the authors.

Reference: Nature paper (15 April)
Go deeper with this Nature News & Views article by cancer researchers Colm O’hUigin and Mary Carrington (8 min read)

Early colorectal cancer has metastatic traits

Early-stage colorectal tumours contain ‘oncofetal cells’ — those with metastatic features — but these cells don’t spread because conditions aren’t yet right. Researchers used human colorectal cancer tissue to generate organoids and studied the traits of cells at different stages in the tumour’s development. They found that early-stage colorectal tumours had cells with embryo-like characteristics that are associated with metastasis. “The fact that most early colorectal cancers show no signs of metastatic seeding, indicates that oncofetal cells may be essential but not sufficient for metastatic success,” report the authors.

Reference: Nature paper (15 April)

Features & opinion

Peptide fad could promote cancer growth

Injectable drugs called peptides made popular by social-media influencers could increase cancer risks, lead to infection or cause heavy metal poisoning. “They’re really just unapproved illegal drugs,” says cell biologist Paul Knoepfler. Many peptides, including CJC-1295, increase levels of growth hormones and encourage tissue growth, while others, such as BPC-157, boost blood-vessel formation — creating several opportunities for tumour growth. Unapproved drugs can also be contaminated with bacteria or heavy metals, says internal medicine physician Shaila Pai-Verma who has seen people with reactions to peptides, such as rashes and poisoning, in her clinic.

The Wall Street Journal | 6 min read (paywall)

The un-silencing of the X chromosome

Around half of the human population, and most women, have two X chromosomes. One of these X chromosomes is silenced early in embryonic development by coating it with an RNA molecule called Xist. The inactive X chromosome, called Xi, still plays a role in supporting growth and most embryos can’t survive without it. Deleting Xist in B cells increases the chances of female mice developing the autoimmune disease lupus erythematosus, and the loss of Xist RNA later in life can trigger a slight increase in the levels of certain proteins, causing aggressive breast tumours to develop.

Nature | 12 min read
 Reference: Cell paper (2022)

Infographic of the week
Brains with tumours had higher concentrations of microplastics and nanoplastics than healthy brains, according to an analysis of tissue samples from more than 100 people. Tumours can compromise the blood-brain barrier, which might help plastics enter the brain, report the researchers. Reference: Nature Health paper (20 April) (Li, R. et al./Nature Health)

Quote of the week

“It’s not that people are suddenly rejecting evidence – it’s that people only recently started using scientific evidence to routinely guide decisions in health and policy, and this is one of many setbacks along the way.”

The recent wave of anti-science sentiment needs to be put into broader historical context, argues author and Nature editor Helen Pearson. The term ‘evidence-based medicine’ was only coined in 1991. Before that, doctors didn’t routinely consult research but instead relied on the opinions of more senior physicians. In historical terms, we’ve come a long way. (The Guardian | 6 min read)

The Kraken — a giant cephalopod of lore — is real. This is not a drill. A paper published in Science this month described a 100-million-year-old octopus fossil that might have been up to 19 metres long and could crunch down on large reptiles with its “powerful jaws”.

Abandon ship and send tips and feedback to cancerbriefing@nature.com.

Thanks for reading,
Felicity Nelson, editor, Nature Briefing: Cancer
With contributions by Jacob Smith
